text: The surgical team proceeds with a transperitoneal approach using the SI robot, with two assistant ports. The colon and small bowel are reflected medially, while the ureter and gonadal vein are retracted laterally. The surgeon follows the psoas muscle to reach the main renal hilum, where vessel loops are placed around the main renal artery and vein, and the kidney is circumferentially mobilized.

The surgeon frees the ureter from the kidney and circumferentially mobilizes it. The kidney is rotated 180 degrees, bringing the posterior surface anterior and the upper pole to the lower pole. An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to score the tumor before proceeding with further surgical steps.
The surgeon clamps the main renal artery and vein, proceeding with dissection to locate the posterior branch of the renal artery that is supplying the tumor.
The surgeon proceeds through the scored area of resection on the kidney, carefully navigating the tissue to assess the extent of the tumor involvement.
The surgeon requests a back or metal clip to secure a structure, then proceeds to mobilize the kidney, indicating manipulation and repositioning within the surgical field.

The right kidney is exposed, with the colon and duodenum visible nearby. The vena cava is also in view, with the liver retracted upwards. The renal vein and artery are identified, along with the ureter and the right adrenal gland. The kidney has been freed from surrounding structures.

The surgeon has freed the kidney and is rotating it by disconnecting the ureter from the lower pole. A small amount of fat is left intentionally as a handle. The kidney is flipped, revealing the upper and lower poles and the posterior surface. The ureter is visible near the kidney.
The surgeon identifies a posterior branch of the renal artery but does not manipulate it. An ultrasound probe is prepared to score the area of the tumor on the kidney.

The surgeon uses ultrasound to score the area around the tumor, marking regions where the tumor is present and where it is absent. The intraoperative picture is requested to visualize the scoring process.

The surgeon examines the rest of the kidney, noting areas that appear clean and free of tumor. The visible tumor is identified and delineated, with the remainder of the kidney appearing healthy and intact.

The surgeon identifies the tumor, marking its boundaries visually. The tumor is visible in the surgical field, and the scoring process is used to delineate the tumor's extent, confirming its removal at specific spots.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound to visualize the tumor boundaries and proceeds to connect marked areas on the kidney, indicating the extent of the tumor removal.

The surgeon identifies the section of the kidney that needs to be removed, noting the artery that runs directly into the area but deciding not to ligate it immediately. The focus is on dissecting the vessels associated with the intrarenal part of the tumor.
The surgeon requests gentle suction and prepares to proceed with the next step of the nephrectomy, indicating the need for a bulldog clamp.
The surgeon places a bulldog clamp on the renal artery and vein to prevent back bleeding while addressing a central hilar tumor. The main renal vein is visible as the surgeon prepares to cut into it.

The surgeon opens the surgical field wider, preparing to clamp the renal vein. The optional use of a bulldog clamp on the renal vein is considered, with a discussion about clamping on the left side of the renal vein.
The surgeon directs an instrument straight towards the tumor, indicating the need to take a branch of an artery. The decision to potentially remove the entire artery is noted but deferred.

The surgical field reveals the central sinus of the kidney with visible fibrotic tissue covering the tumor. The surgeon identifies a potential additional arterial branch obstructing the operation of the fourth robotic arm.
The surgeon attempts to spare a structure by placing a wet clip, rotating it 180 degrees before securing it in place.
The surgeon applies a wet clip to the tissue, then removes the scissors from the surgical field before proceeding. The team confirms the placement with a visual check.
The surgeon examines the surgical field, identifying a capsular breach near the tumor and the collecting system. The left arm is maneuvered to assist in the procedure from a different angle.
The surgeon points to an area identified as a tumor, highlighting a significant mass within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small tumor thrombus adjacent to an artery, highlighting the anatomical structures involved in the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses suction to clear the tumor thrombus from the vein, ensuring the area is free of obstruction.
The surgeon examines an artery that is visually connected to the tumor, assessing whether it can be preserved. The decision is made to clip the artery as it is directly involved with the tumor.
The surgeon prepares to remove the tumor, ensuring comfortable margins around it. The vascularity is noted as a concern, and a surgical bag is used for containment.
The surgeon identifies the thrombus and prepares for further excision. The needle driver is requested as the team continues the tumor excision process.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to anchor the vein with a couple of stitches before unclamping it, ensuring stability in the surgical field.
The surgeon unclamps the vein to observe any bleeding, preparing to suture the bleeding sites. The search for a suture cut needle driver is unsuccessful.
The surgeon unclamps the vein, observing the kidney as it regains a pink color, indicating successful hemostasis due to the clips maintaining arterial supply.

The surgeon demonstrates strategic placement of wet clips on the kidney, noting that only fifteen percent of the kidney has been sutured. Despite minimal suturing, effective hemostasis is achieved by clipping larger intrarenal vessels in advance. The kidney is now unclamped, reducing ischemia time significantly. The surgeon plans to undersow each wet clip to prevent migration into the collecting system.
The surgeon requests a cut and observes the kidney, noting that one part is pink while another is not. A stitch is placed, and the surgeon asks for a 3-0 suture.

The surgeon requests an SH 130 needle and suction, followed by irrigation of the surgical field. The concern is expressed about the potential for suturing to disrupt underlying blood vessels, emphasizing the importance of maintaining hemostasis.
The surgeon requests the removal of a wet clip and instructs the urology resident to pull the urethral catheter down by five centimeters, ensuring proper positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to close off the collecting system, ensuring that the surgical area is secure.
The surgeon continues the procedure using irrigation to clear the surgical field, noting the presence of bleeding as a positive indicator of tissue perfusion.

The surgeon observes venous oozing in the surgical field, which is not significant. The decision is made to use a bolster for support rather than additional suturing, considering the kidney's condition.
The surgeon strategically places stitches in the kidney tissue, ensuring adequate closure. The tissue appears pink, indicating healthy perfusion. Posterior branches are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon places multiple bolster stitches, using a two-zero suture for substantial support. Suction is applied to remove clots from the surgical field, and flow seal is prepared for use. The tumor remains outside the bag due to the absence of an automatic opening bag, prioritizing time efficiency.
The surgeon places a stitch with the needle tip emerging from a lower point to ensure good compression with the bolster, while the tumor remains outside the bag.
The surgeon uses the left hand to take substantial bites with the needle, ensuring the stitches do not cut through the tissue. The sectioning continues to maintain the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a bolster to ensure no leakage from the closed area of the collecting system, followed by suctioning and the application of flow seal to manage any potential fluid escape.

The video shows the thrombus adjacent to the tumor, which is in contact with the central sinus. The intact capsule of the tumor is visible, along with the collecting system. The surgeon highlights the minimal parenchyma loss, primarily located on top of the tumor, with additional devascularized tissue from the extraction process.

The surgeon uses ultrasound to score the tumor, deepening the scoring circumferentially to free up the area. The surgical approach progresses from the hilum towards the kidney, following the main renal artery. Branches of the renal artery are taken only after confirming that the posterior branch supplies the entire tumor and cannot be salvaged. Suction is applied during the procedure.

The surgeon applies wet clips during the resection, avoiding the need for sutures in most areas. Only specific regions receive stitches, while the wet clips help minimize the need for extensive suturing. Blue adhesive is applied to secure the area.

The surgeon adjusts the white bolster tie to ensure clarity and efficiency in stitch placement. The line of demarcation on the kidney has shrunk, and the tissue appears pink, indicating good perfusion. The kidney is flipped back to potentially improve perfusion further.

The surgeon carefully pulls the stitch vertically up and away to avoid tearing the tissue, while applying a wet clip to secure the area. The stitch is cinched down gently to ensure it is tight enough without cutting through the tissue.
The surgeon requests a cut and flips the kidney back to ensure good hemostasis. They emphasize removing needles one at a time to avoid losing them.

The surgeon requests suction and flips the kidney over to examine the opposite side, observing the tissue's pink appearance, indicating good perfusion. The camera is cleaned to ensure clear visibility.
The surgeon examines the front side of the kidney, noting the presence of a blue adhesive material. Suction irrigation is recommended using a disposable striker suction device.

The surgeon requests a four by eighteen gauze to mop up excess fluid while suctioning continues. A ureteral catheter is used to better identify the ureter during dissection, allowing for intraoperative injection and confirmation.

The surgeon assesses the vascularity of the kidney, noting the lower pole appears healthy with good blood supply. The posterior area shows a blue discoloration, while the upper part and anteriorly look normal. A nephropexy stitch is requested to secure the kidney.
The surgeon observes a hematoma on the kidney and emphasizes the importance of placing two nephropexy stitches to prevent torquing and potential infarction of the kidney.
The surgeon places another stitch, preparing to insert a drain. A V-lock suture is used for the stitching.
